title: Forced Swim Test — Claim Defensibility
subtitle: Is the forced swim test claim defensible enough to anchor the film's opening?
scope: One claim from How to Make Drugs and Feel Great About Everything: the forced swim test — used to evaluate antidepressants on rodents — is scientifically discredited but still in use.
sourceCoverage: Partial — first expert interview on file; primary NIH source attached. Counter-voice and legal review still open.

contentSources.name: NIH NCBI page on the forced swim test
Interview — Dr. Neal Barnard, MD, FACC (PCRM)
Pew Research 2018 survey — attitudes toward animal testing

objective: Determine whether the forced-swim-test claim is defensible enough — across testimony, documents, footage, counter-voices, and legal risk — to anchor the film's opening sequence.
successCondition: The claim has reached at least counter-voiced in the defensibility lifecycle, with named-expert testimony, primary documentary evidence, deliberately included opposing voice, and a legal-risk read on file.

objectiveFacets.name: Defensibility lifecycle
objectiveFacets.description: Move the claim from untested toward locked-in-cut through observable evidence.

objectiveFacets.name: Expert testimony
objectiveFacets.description: Named, credentialed researchers on camera explaining the test and its standing.

objectiveFacets.name: Primary documentary evidence
objectiveFacets.description: Source material on NIH/regulatory pages and peer-reviewed critique of the test's validity.

objectiveFacets.name: Counter-voice
objectiveFacets.description: A defender of the test's continued use, deliberately included before the refutation lands.

objectiveFacets.name: Personal-frame bridge
objectiveFacets.description: Filmmaker's depression / antidepressant decision threads into the claim.

objectiveFacets.name: Legal-risk read
objectiveFacets.description: AETA / libel exposure for naming labs, journals, or pharma actors.

invariants.name: Claim has defensibility status
invariants.statement: The claim card carries a defensibility lifecycle status at all times.

invariants.name: Lock requires counter-voice
invariants.statement: The claim cannot move to locked-in-cut without at least one deliberately-included counter-voice on record.

invariants.name: Proof rungs escalate
invariants.statement: Proof-ladder rungs are ordered weakest to strongest; a higher rung's status does not exceed the strongest satisfied lower rung.

callout.title: Progress so far
items: Barnard interview recorded; NIH source attached. Lifecycle moved from untested → testimony-only → corroborated.
Counter-voice not yet identified. Legal-risk read still pending.

data.name: Forced swim test
data.claim: The forced swim test, used to evaluate antidepressants on rodents, is scientifically discredited but still in use as a comparative tool.
data.personalFrameLink: Tied to the filmmaker's question of whether to take antidepressants developed via this method.
legalRiskNotes: Initial counsel read not yet on file. Naming Barnard is low-risk — credentialed source on record. Naming specific pharma actors using the test would need clearance.
openGaps: No counter-voice identified — defender of the test's continued use needs to be approached.
notes: 2026-05-04: First interview recorded with Dr. Barnard at PCRM offices.
2026-05-12: NIH NCBI page describing the forced swim test attached as primary source.

data.name: Test is named as a standard antidepressant comparator on a primary regulatory source
notes: NIH NCBI page describes the forced swim test as a quantification method used to compare antidepressants. Establishes the claim's lower bound — the test is at least *used*.

data.name: Credentialed researcher on camera states the test has been discredited
notes: Barnard interview recorded but not yet edited; needs the specific quote isolated and the credential lower-third confirmed.

data.name: Test originated as a 1970s quantification proxy, not a model of human depression
notes: Per Barnard interview: the test emerged because researchers wanted to put a number on depression behaviour, not because the swim behaviour modelled depression itself.

data.name: Personal-frame thread under strain
data.riskSummary: Once the claim adds technical density (mechanism, statistics, protocols), the filmmaker's first-person voiceover may feel grafted on rather than connective.
data.nextCheck: Review opening voiceover after Barnard segment is rough-cut; confirm the depression / antidepressant decision still threads through.
evidence: Current draft VO bridges from personal stake to the test in under 90s, which works while only one expert speaks. Risk grows when more experts are added.

data.name: NIH NCBI — Forced Swim Test reference page
data.author: NCBI
notes: Primary regulatory source naming the test as a comparator. Establishes the lower-bound proof rung.

data.name: Pew Research — Public attitudes toward animal testing (2018)
data.author: Pew Research Center
notes: Used to frame audience baseline (47% favor / 52% oppose) — context, not direct evidence for the claim.
